title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Storms trigger Warren moratorium; Saline fights massive data center over water and farmland.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Monday, July 20, and we're diving into how recent storms are pushing communities to hit the brakes on new projects.
Exactly, and that's our big story today.

Let's start with Warren in Macomb County. The city council approved a temporary moratorium on new data centers after July storms knocked down more than 60 power lines and exposed the strain on the aging grid.
That sounds serious. What exactly happened with those storms, and why is it leading to a freeze now?
The storms hit hard, downing dozens of lines and leaving parts of the city without power. City leaders say the grid just isn't ready for the massive electricity demands these facilities bring.
And they're pausing new approvals while attorneys draft the details and zoning rules get revised. How long could this last?

It's temporary for now, but it gives them time to figure out stronger protections. This isn't the first place hitting pause—other townships have done similar things when growth outpaces infrastructure.

Right, and it ties into the bigger picture of communities waking up to these projects. Take Saline Township in Washtenaw County—residents there are pushing back hard against a $16 billion Stargate data center already under construction.
Yeah, it's a farming community of about 2,400 people, and locals say the project will drain water supplies, drive up utility costs, and wipe out the rural character they've known for generations.
Water and power are the big concerns everywhere. How does something this size even get approved without those questions answered upfront?
That's part of the fight. The project is moving forward, but residents are organizing, and it highlights how fast these deals can advance before locals fully grasp the trade-offs.
And on top of that, Saline Township dropped its $4.8 billion cap on incentives for the Oracle-OpenAI campus, handing the company an unlimited tax break. What does that mean for taxpayers?

It means decades of lost property tax revenue with no upper limit. The reversal came after the project was already in motion, and residents are left footing the bill while the company gets a blank check.
That feels like a huge shift. Was there pushback during the negotiations?
There was, but the cap came off anyway. It shows how incentives can escalate quickly when big tech is involved.

Meanwhile, over in Ingham County, Mason residents are raising cash today for a fundraiser to fight a proposed data center. They're worried about noise, power strain, and losing farmland right near their homes.
It's another sign of organized resistance in mid-Michigan. These aren't isolated complaints anymore—groups are pooling resources and getting more strategic.

And yesterday Governor Whitmer rolled out the Michigan Affordable and Responsible Growth Action Plan with new siting, power, and water standards. Companies that skip the rules could lose state incentives.
The plan lands right as local pushback is growing over bills and farmland loss. It aims to set some guardrails statewide.
But the House Speaker is still holding up a separate bill that would add more statewide rules on siting, power, and tax breaks. Sponsors say the delay leaves townships without tools.
That bill had bipartisan support before the stall. Without it, local governments are left handling these on their own, often after projects are already proposed.

In southwest Michigan, Dowagiac residents filed a lawsuit yesterday claiming nonstop noise from a hyperscale data center is disrupting daily life. The case shines a light on weak noise protections during approvals.
Exactly. Small communities are realizing these facilities can change everything once they're running.
And in Kalamazoo County, Vicksburg residents rallied on Saturday against planned projects, citing noise, traffic, and utility strain. More actions are planned if proposals move forward.
Peaceful protests are becoming more common as word spreads.

Then there's the public hearing this week in Kent County where the utility will explain how it will serve Microsoft's first major AI campus. Neighbors are already questioning the added grid and water pressure.
West Michigan's first big AI facility is drawing early scrutiny.

And in Augusta Township near Ann Arbor, voters face a referendum next month on a large project after weeks of protests over traffic, noise, and power. A resident even urged officials to tune out social media pressure.
That vote could set a precedent for how these decisions play out at the ballot box.
So pulling it together, Graham—what's really driving all this resistance right now?

It's the combination of visible impacts like power outages and noise, plus the long-term costs to water, farmland, and local taxes. People are seeing the scale and wanting more say before ground is broken.
And with storms exposing grid weaknesses, places like Warren are acting fast. Do you think we'll see more moratoriums this summer?
It's possible. As more communities review their infrastructure, pauses give time to update rules. The question is whether state standards will catch up or if locals keep leading the way.
What about the companies—how are they responding to this pushback?

They're still moving on big projects, but the unlimited incentives in Saline and the Warren freeze show the risks when local support erodes. Compliance with new state rules could become key to keeping incentives.
And for everyday residents, what changes first—higher bills or lost farmland?
Both are on the table. Power strain can hit bills quickly, while farmland loss is more permanent. That's why these fights are heating up in rural and suburban areas alike.
Looking ahead, how might the governor's plan interact with these local actions?
It could give communities leverage if companies want incentives, but the stalled House bill means enforcement might still fall to townships for now.
And the lawsuits like in Dowagiac—could they slow other projects?
They put pressure on approvals and highlight gaps in noise rules. Other residents are watching closely.
So the takeaway is that organized local efforts are reshaping how these projects move forward.
Yes, and that's why we're tracking every township meeting and vote.
Now for quick hits. Starting with the Mason fundraiser today—residents are raising cash specifically to fight the Ingham County proposal over noise and farmland.
It's a concrete step showing how opposition is turning into organized campaigns.
Yesterday's Whitmer plan sets clear standards, with incentives on the line for non-compliance.
It arrives at a moment when local concerns are already loud.
The Dowagiac lawsuit puts a spotlight on noise after approvals.
Residents say daily life has already changed.
Vicksburg's Saturday rally drew dozens protesting noise, traffic, and utilities in Kalamazoo County.
Organizers say more actions are coming.
The House Speaker's hold on the guardrail bill leaves townships without extra tools.
Bipartisan support was there before the delay.
Kent County's utility hearing this week will address power and water for the Microsoft AI campus.
Neighbors are already voicing grid concerns.
Augusta Township's referendum next month lets voters decide on a project near Ann Arbor.
After heated protests and warnings about local impacts.
On deck, listeners should check the Michigan Data Center Tracker for any township meetings this week—agendas often include zoning reviews or public comments on proposals.
No major ones listed for today, but things can pop up quickly.
We'll keep an eye on the Warren moratorium drafting and the Kent hearing.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Warren Freezes New Data Centers After Storms; Saline Residents Resist $16B Stargate Projec
